channel.title: Kubernetes Workshop
channel.copyright: Made with ❤️ by Polar Squad. Content on this site is licensed under a Creative Commons license

item.title: Introduction
item.description: This Kubernetes workshop goal is to teach reader basics of Kubernetes and how you can use it. Start by cloning the workshop material git clone https://github.

item.title: Install Kubernetes
item.description: We will start installing Kubernetes. There’s multiple ways to run Kubernetes, select what suites best for you.

item.title: Install with Docker for Mac with Kubernetes
item.description: If you’re using Docker for Mac, the installation is easy. Install Edge version of Docker for Mac from here Turn on experimental Kubernetes support Click Docker icon at top right corner Click Preferences Select Kubernetes tab Select Enable Kubernetes Click Apply Wait until installation completes! Test Kubernetes ~ $ kubectl get nodes NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION docker-for-desktop Ready master 3m v1.

item.title: Installation on Linux
item.description: If you’re using Linux, you can use Minikube for running Kubernetes. In this guide, we’re going to show how to run Kubernetes on top of a KVM-based virtual machine using Minikube. First, make sure you have Docker installed in Linux. Here’s a list of guides for the officially supported distributions. Ubuntu Fedora (for Fedora 28 you need to enable test repository for now) Debian CentOS If you wish to manage Docker as a non-root user, add yourself to the Docker group.

item.title: Pod
item.description: Pod Pod is a group of one or more tightly related containers that will run together on the same worker node and in the same Linux namespace(s). Each pod has its own IP, Hostname, processes etc. running a single application and/or additional supporting processes, each running in its own container. Pods are spread out on different worker nodes. Good practise is to have frontend and backend containers in different pods. This way utilization of your infrastructure is more efficient.

item.title: ReplicaSet, Services and LoadBalancer
item.description: In this example we have Node.js web application which accept’s HTTP requests and responses with the hostname of the machine it is running in. The directory apps/kubers/ in the workshop Git repo contains the Node.JS source code for the apps, Dockerfile to build the Docker images, and Kubernetes deployment configuration files. You can find pre-built images from Polar Squad Docker Hub ReplicaSet Usually one does not create pods directly but create other resources like ReplicaSet’s which creates and manages pods.

item.title: Deployment
item.description: Deployment Deployment is used for higher-level resource to deploy applications and update them declaratively. Creating Deployment a ReplicaSet resource is created “underneath”. Actual pods are created and managed by ReplicaSets not by Deployment directly. workshop $ kubectl apply -f examples/deployment-frontend.yaml service "frontend" created workshop $ kubectl apply -f examples/deployment-backend.yaml service "backend" created workshop $ kubectl get deployments NAME DESIRED CURRENT UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE backend 1 1 1 1 32s frontend 1 1 1 1 36s workshop $ kubectl rollout status deployment frontend deployment "frontend" successfully rolled out workshop $ kubectl rollout status deployment backend deployment "backend" successfully rolled out workshop $ kubectl get pods -o wide NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE backend-759bc76ddc-czxlp 1/1 Running 0 2m 10.

item.title: Useful Links
item.description: The Kubernetes website The Kubernetes Blog The Kubernetes community’s Slack channel Kubernetes source code Could Native Computing Foundation’s Youtube channel
